The crazy part about Andrew Left's story is that he himself through one single tweet was able to move a stock like $NVDA, $TSLA, $PLTR, at the time, anywhere between 5-20%. The market caps then were still very sizeable at $250B - $500B ish I believe.

It's one thing to move a microcap low floater, it's another to move a mega cap established company.

Anyways, he'll get out of jail in 2-4 years ish max. A movie like Wolf of Wall Street 2 will be made about him. He'll be infamous and famous, will still have money when he gets out like Jordan Belfort, he'll be fine.

Now, sell me this pen. IYKYK.

tweets.text:
“For me, I like to keep it simple.

There are only two regions in the world with “real” excess crude oil inventories today: China and the US.

China is a black box. No one even knows how much oil inventories it has. It doesn’t publish its data, so all 3rd-party providers are just guessing. We can estimate how much it has, but only after the fact.

I propose a simpler solution: follow the US.

Let me explain.

Since the onset of the conflict, oil barrel counters knew that the US would be the last place to draw. If you’ve been in oil long enough, you know why. The US, thanks to the US shale revolution, is in a particularly interesting situation.

US shale produces crude of a quality not needed by US domestic refineries. It produces ultra-light sweet crude, whereas US refineries require heavy/medium sour crude. Imagine playing tennis with a golf ball, and golf with a tennis ball. They are both balls, but they are not in the right sport. You can kind of get the gist.

US also benefits from landlocked Canadian heavy oil. With Venezuela as an ally now, the US also has another source of heavy crude. Because the barrels received from Canada and Venezuela usually have an API gravity below 25, we need to blend these barrels with condensate. The remaining excess gets exported. This is also another reason why the Trump administration should never ban US crude exports.

The only reason US commercial crude storage would drop is that there’s “demand” for the barrels. If there was low demand for the barrels, US commercial crude storage would build, all things equal. This is why you didn’t see a decline in US commercial crude storage at the start of the Iran conflict.

But this all changed by early April. When I wrote that the oil market breaking point was mid-April, it was because we knew from the deficit math that the world would start pulling US commercial crude storage lower. 

The US is releasing ~10 million bbls a week now in the SPR, and all of that implies the US is the last place with excess crude.

So the moment US commercial crude storage started to decline, I knew it was just a race against time. The demand pull would drop US commercial crude storage to an operational minimum, and the chaos would unfold. Once US crude exports get priced out, the rest of the world will suffer. The marginal barrel is gone, so everyone will have to compete for that extra 1-2 million b/d of crude. It adds up quickly.

The timing for US commercial crude storage to hit operational minimum is late July. But the market will need to price out US crude exports mid-June.”

tweets.text:
China's crude oil inventory adjusted for imbalance.

Satellite data captures 50% of onshore inventories. Zero chance the data is what it is. They also have guardrails in place to prevent errenous data like a massive 30 million bbl decrease.

People taking satellite data at face value will see that it is revised retroactively and without warning.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
a couple things from riding in waymos in miami this weekend:

def felt the waymo drove very safe.  didn't feel in danger once.

once we hit a certain % (prob like 20-25%) of self driving cars on the road, i think it will start to alleviate traffic congestion.  and then traffic will get significantly better the higher that number goes.

will prob significantly increase the number of electronics/components/memory used in cars, as everything will have sensors both inside and out.

the form factor of cars will definitely be changing.  i would've preferred being able to recline the seat more so that i could lay down or relax more.  also will change the nature of commuting.  i bet there will be workout cars, so people can work out on their commute to or from work.

i bet autonomous car acceptance happens faster than robot acceptance.  not as creepy.

you should prob try and buy a bunch of these when they rollout and put them into service.  i bet a utilization rate even as low as 50% would make these things insanely profitable.

tweets.text:
just speak a lot… I met a French girl on the Transsiberian train once, and she wouldn’t shut up… spoke to everyone in English, French, Russian all the time… but that’s how she became very fluent in all 3 languages so lesson in there 

when you vocalize something in actual context, you simply remember it for good 

apart from that, really depends on how you like to learn… I am grammar person for example so dissecting sentence structures works best for me but other people prefer reading or listening to a lot of content etc
tweets.full_text:

apart from that, really depends on how you like to learn… I am grammar person for example so dissecting sentence structures works best for me but other people prefer reading or listening to a lot of content etc

tweets.text:
How are prediction markets spinning this vanilla ‘to win’ bet on the Knicks by some bar as innovation?  Actually crazy the spin zones these guys amplify to try and seem like righteous ambassadors of sports technology.  

That same exact bet could have been executed in the same exact way for the last 500 years…. 

Prediction markets are literally just GAMBLING the same as everything else.
